title: Nahum 3
subtitle: Woe to the City of Blood

sections.header: Verses 1-7 — "Full of Lies, Full of Plunder"

heb.paragraph: The plural damim (literally 'bloods') in 3:1 is the standard Hebrew term for bloodguilt and violent death. The phrase ʻir damim ('city of bloods') is a formal indictment: Nineveh's entire civic identity is constituted by violence. The same phrase is applied to Jerusalem in Ezekiel 22:2 and 24:6, demonstrating that YHWH applies identical standards of justice to pagan empires and to his own covenant people.

The noun keshaphim (from kashah, 'to practice sorcery') in 3:4 adds religious deception to Nineveh's crimes. Assyrian state religion included extensive divination practices, omen reading, and ritual manipulation. Nahum characterizes this as 'prostitution' — the enslaving of nations through a combination of military force and ideological seduction.

refs.note: Ezekiel uses the identical phrase 'city of blood' against Jerusalem, demonstrating that YHWH judges his own people by the same standard applied to Nineveh.

The 'great prostitute' of Revelation, associated with Babylon, perpetuates the prophetic tradition Nahum establishes: imperial power sustained by violence, wealth, and deception is a form of harlotry.

notes.note: The woe (hoi) is a funeral cry: Nahum is singing Nineveh's death dirge before the city has fallen. The three charges — blood, lies, plunder — summarize the entire Assyrian imperial system. This was not an empire built on commerce or culture but on systematic violence, deception, and theft.

The staccato imagery of battle — whips, wheels, horses, chariots, swords, spears, casualties — is deliberately overwhelming. The poet forces the reader to experience the chaos and horror that Assyria inflicted on others now turned against itself.

The prostitute metaphor identifies the spiritual dimension of Assyrian imperialism. The 'sorceries' include the elaborate divination system by which Assyrian kings claimed divine authorization for their conquests. God strips away this pretense of divine sanction.

The public humiliation of Nineveh mirrors Assyrian treatment of conquered peoples. The question 'Who will mourn for her?' anticipates the answer: no one. A nation that showed no compassion receives none.

Calvin observes that God permits empires to accumulate power through violence precisely to demonstrate his sovereignty when he destroys them. Nineveh's blood, lies, and plunder are simultaneously her crimes and the evidence presented at her trial.

The exposure of the prostitute is not mere punishment but revelation: God strips away the attractive surface of imperial power to expose the corruption beneath. The nations that were 'enslaved' by Nineveh's allure now see her true nature and recoil.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The woe form (hoi) functions as both a prophetic indictment and a funerary lament, combining legal accusation with the emotional weight of mourning over a doomed city.
The phrase 'mistress of sorceries' (baʻalat keshaphim) recalls the Akkadian belet keshapti, a formal title. Nahum may be deliberately inverting Assyrian honorific language.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: The woe oracle follows the classical prophetic form: accusation (vv. 1, 4), evidence (vv. 2-3), and implied sentence. The rapid-fire battle imagery of verses 2-3 functions simultaneously as description of Assyrian warfare (what Nineveh has done) and prediction of Nineveh's fate (what will be done to her).

The divine first-person speech ('I am against you') repeats the formula from 2:13, creating a structural parallel between the two halves of the book. The punishment of exposure uses the specific language of covenant-curse traditions found in treaty texts from Esarhaddon and other Assyrian treaties — an ironic application of Assyria's own diplomatic instruments against itself.

hist.context: The woe oracle (hoi) of chapter 3 functions as the formal indictment in YHWH's lawsuit against Nineveh. The charges are comprehensive: military violence ('city of blood'), diplomatic treachery ('full of lies'), economic exploitation ('full of plunder'), and religious manipulation ('sorceries'). The battle sequence of verses 2-3 — whips cracking, wheels clattering, horses galloping, swords flashing, bodies piling — may describe either a typical Assyrian military campaign or the battle that will end Nineveh itself. The 'prostitute' metaphor (vv. 4-7) draws on a well-established prophetic tradition (cf. Isa 23:15-17; Ezek 16, 23) but applies it uniquely to a foreign imperial power rather than to Israel. The punishment of public exposure (v. 5) mirrors the Assyrian practice of humiliating defeated peoples through deportation, stripping, and public display — a deliberate ironic reversal.

heb.paragraph: No Amon (literally 'city of Amon') is the Egyptian city of Thebes, modern Luxor. The reference is to its sack by the Assyrian king Ashurbanipal in 663 BC. The irony is devastating: Nineveh's own greatest military triumph — the conquest of the supposedly impregnable Thebes — becomes the proof that no city is safe from divine judgment, including Nineveh itself.

refs.note: Isaiah's oracle against Egypt and Cush anticipates the vulnerability of African powers that Nahum cites as historical evidence. If YHWH can defeat Egypt, he can defeat Assyria.

Amos uses a similar rhetorical strategy, challenging Israel to compare itself with fallen cities: 'Go to Calneh and look at it; go to Hamath the great; then go down to Gath of the Philistines. Are they better than these two kingdoms?'

notes.note: The fall of Thebes was the high-water mark of Assyrian military power. By pointing to it, Nahum demolishes Nineveh's confidence in its own invincibility. If the greatest city in Africa could be conquered, what makes Nineveh think it is exempt? The very brutality Assyria inflicted on Thebes — dashed infants, enslaved nobles — will be visited upon Assyria itself.

The image of ripe figs falling into the eater's mouth is a powerful metaphor for the ease of Nineveh's conquest. Despite its massive fortifications, the city will offer no real resistance. When God has decreed a city's fall, even its strongest defenses become laughably weak.

God here teaches through historical example. The destruction of Thebes, accomplished by Assyria itself, proves that no earthly power is permanent. Calvin notes that God often uses the very instruments of a tyrant's success as evidence at his trial: 'You conquered Thebes; now learn from your own conquest that you too can be conquered.'

The metaphor of figs falling into the mouth conveys effortless victory. When God fights against a city, its walls become paper, its soldiers become straw. This humbles all human confidence in military technology.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: No-Amon (Thebes) was situated on both banks of the Nile in Upper Egypt. The city's strategic position, with the river serving as a natural moat, made it one of the most defensible cities in the ancient world.

The treatment of Thebes' population — infanticide, enslavement, and lot-casting for prisoners — follows the standard Assyrian practice documented in royal annals and depicted in palace reliefs at Nineveh itself.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: The Thebes analogy constitutes an a fortiori argument: if Thebes fell despite superior natural defenses (the Nile) and powerful allies (Cush, Put, Egypt), how much more will Nineveh fall when YHWH himself is the attacker? The rhetorical force depends on Ashurbanipal's sack of Thebes in 663 BC being within living memory, providing the strongest possible terminus post quem for the book's composition.

The series of comparisons — drunk, hiding, fig trees, women, open gates — systematically strips away every layer of Nineveh's military confidence. Each metaphor targets a different dimension of defensive capacity: courage (drunk), fortification (fig trees), manpower (women), strategic position (open gates).

hist.context: The Thebes comparison (vv. 8-10) is Nahum's most pointed historical argument. Thebes (No-Amon) was the ancient religious capital of Upper Egypt, protected by the Nile's natural defenses and backed by the military resources of Cush (Nubia), Put (Libya), and Egypt itself. Yet Ashurbanipal conquered it in 663 BC, an event within living memory of Nahum's audience. The logic is inescapable: if Thebes, with all its natural and military advantages, fell to Assyria, then Assyria itself — whose God is far mightier than Egypt's — is equally vulnerable. The descriptions of Thebes' fall (infants dashed, nobles in chains, lots cast for prisoners) mirror standard Assyrian siege practices attested in royal inscriptions and palace reliefs. Verses 11-13 draw out the implications: Nineveh will be drunk with defeat, its fortresses will fall like ripe figs, its troops will prove as ineffective as women in ancient military reckoning.

heb.paragraph: The adjective kehah in 3:19 describes a wound beyond healing. This is the final theological verdict: Nineveh's condition is terminal. Unlike Jonah's Nineveh, which received a reprieve through repentance, Nahum's Nineveh has exhausted divine patience. The root also appears in Jeremiah 10:19 and 14:17 for Judah's own wounds, demonstrating the universality of divine judgment.

refs.note: Jeremiah uses identical 'incurable wound' language for Judah, demonstrating that YHWH applies the same diagnostic categories to all nations. Healing is withheld from those whose sin has become systemic.
The rejoicing of the nations at Nineveh's fall (Nah 3:19) prefigures the heavenly command in Revelation: 'Rejoice over her, you heavens! Rejoice, you people of God!'
Joel's locust imagery provides the agricultural template that Nahum adapts for military-political purposes: successive waves of destruction that leave nothing behind.

notes.note: The ironic commands to prepare for siege underscore the futility of human resistance against divine decree. God is not indifferent to military strength; he simply overrides it. When he determines to destroy, no wall, no moat, no army can prevent it.

The locust imagery is triply apt: locusts were a dreaded plague in the ancient Near East, Assyria's merchants had spread across the known world like a swarm, and the disappearing guards reveal that those who profited from Nineveh's power will abandon her at the first sign of trouble.

The funeral dirge for the king of Assyria is the book's climax. His shepherds (generals) sleep the sleep of death, his flock (people) are scattered without a shepherd, his wound is fatal and beyond remedy. The universal clapping at his fall reveals the depth of suffering Assyria had inflicted on the world.

Calvin reads the ironic military commands as God's mockery of human self-sufficiency. The command to fortify is given precisely because fortification is futile. God announces what men should do while simultaneously declaring that it will accomplish nothing.

The sleeping shepherds are dead leaders whose neglect ensured their people's destruction. Calvin draws the pastoral lesson: when leaders sleep in spiritual complacency, the flock is scattered. The universal clapping is not cruelty but justice: all who have suffered under tyranny rightly rejoice when the tyrant falls.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The three locust terms — yeleq (locust nymph), ʾarbeh (swarming locust), and gobai (locust swarm) — may represent different developmental stages of the same species or different species entirely. The accumulation of terms intensifies the imagery of overwhelming, irresistible consumption.

The final question is rhetorical and expects a universal negative: no one has escaped Assyria's cruelty. The word raʻa ('cruelty' or 'evil') encompasses both moral wickedness and the concrete suffering it inflicts.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: The locust metaphor undergoes a remarkable reversal across these verses. Initially, the locusts represent the destroyer of Nineveh (v. 15b). Then Nineveh's own merchants and officials are compared to locusts (vv. 16-17) — but with the crucial difference that these locusts fly away, abandoning the city. The metaphor thus captures both the destruction inflicted on Nineveh and the desertion of those who should defend it.

The shift to direct address of the 'king of Assyria' creates a dramatic closing frame. The 'sleeping shepherds' employ the death-as-sleep metaphor common in ancient Near Eastern royal rhetoric, while the 'scattered people on the mountains' reverses the Assyrian deportation policy. The final verse functions as an inclusio with 1:1: the oracle against Nineveh reaches its conclusion with universal recognition of the justice of YHWH's verdict.

hist.context: The final section combines ironic military counsel (vv. 14-15a) with a series of devastating comparisons (vv. 15b-17) and a concluding funeral dirge (vv. 18-19). The command to 'draw water for the siege' and 'strengthen your defenses' is bitter mockery: no preparation can avert the judgment already decreed. The locust imagery (vv. 15-17) develops in three stages: Nineveh's population will be consumed like crops devoured by locusts (v. 15b); her merchants, though numerous as locusts, will strip the land and fly away (v. 16); her guards and officials are themselves like locusts that disappear when the sun rises (v. 17). The final verses address the 'king of Assyria' directly, pronouncing a funeral lament: his shepherds (military leaders) are asleep in death, his people scattered beyond recovery, and all the nations he once oppressed now celebrate his downfall. The final question — 'Who has not felt your endless cruelty?' — serves as the universal indictment that justifies the universal rejoicing.

themes.note: Chapter 3 reaches the highest concentration of judgment and justice themes in the book. The woe oracle, historical argument, and funeral dirge all serve a single theological purpose: demonstrating that YHWH's justice is universal, impartial, and inescapable.

rows.text: Historical Argument: The Fall of Thebes

rows.label: vv.11-13
rows.text: Application: Nineveh Will Share Thebes’ Fate

rows.label: vv.18-19
rows.text: Funeral Dirge for the King of Assyria

lit.note: Chapter 3 completes the oracle with a formal indictment (woe), historical precedent (Thebes), ironic counsel, and funeral dirge. The structure moves from accusation to sentence to lament, closing the prophetic case against Nineveh.

hebtext.note: The plural damim (literally 'bloods') in 3:1 is the standard Hebrew term for bloodguilt and violent death. Assyrian state religion included extensive divination practices, omen reading, and ritual manipulation. ppl.name: Nahum
ppl.text: The woe (hoi) is a funeral cry: Nahum is singing Nineveh's death dirge before the city has fallen…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Woe to the City of Blood

translations.text: “I am against you,” declares the Lord Almighty. “I will lift your skirts over your face. I will show the nations your nakedness and the kingdoms your shame.
Behold, I am against you, declares the LORD of hosts, and will lift up your skirts over your face; and I will make nations look at your nakedness and kingdoms at your shame.
Cush and Egypt were her boundless strength; Put and Libya were among her allies.
Cush was her strength; Egypt too, and that without limit; Put and the Libyans were her helpers.

debate: The final question is rhetorical and expects a universal negative: no one has escaped Assyria's cruelty. The word raʻa ('cruelty' or 'evil') encompass…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Woe to the City of Blood within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.anchor: Woe to the City of Blood
thread.text: The rejoicing of the nations at Nineveh's fall (Nah 3:19) prefigures the heavenly command in Revelation: 'Rejoice over her, you heavens! Rejoice, you people of God!'

coaching.tip: 'Woe to the bloody city, all full of lies and plunder' (v. 1). The indictment accumulates: corpses, heaps of dead, bodies without end. This is Assyria's harvest — they sowed violence across empires and now reap it. The graphic imagery matches Assyrian atrocity reports. Nahum describes their empire's signature style turned against them.

'Are you better than Thebes?' (v. 8). Thebes (No-Amon), Egypt's greatest city, fell to Assyria in 663 BC. If Thebes couldn't resist, why should Nineveh expect immunity? Nahum uses Assyria's own victory as evidence against them. History teaches: empires fall. What Assyria did to others, others will do to Assyria.

prayer_prompt: As Nahum declares that nothing can heal Nineveh's wound, consider asking God whether there are consequences in history that should sober you — and whether the empires you admire are as permanent as they appear.
